item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
That's not how copyright works. A photo of a copyrighted painting is still copyrighted. The Flickr user has engaged in what is called "License Laundering".

It's still copyright of Magritte, and will continue to be until January 1 2038 (since Magritte died in 1967).

Do any images exist that are both suitably creepy and suitably licensed, maybe, but this isn't one of them.

item.title: Re: What is this?
item.content:encoded:
According to the Wikipedia page for the actual painting:

This painting depicts an almost photo-realistic table setting with a slice of ham in the center.

There's your answer!

item.title: What is this?
item.content:encoded: Is it a pancake or a piece of ham? I need answers

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Edit: image removed per below, and is pending replacement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This image is really unnerving which is good since the anomaly deals with visual contact to the painting.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since nobody else did, I just wanted to show my appreciation for calling the team that performed an art theft "Pink Panther".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one gave me nightmares when I was 14.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment being wall mounted in Gallery 27 seems to imply this is on display; mind-screw just hanging out there for any staff to get accidentally exposed to. Seems an unnecessary risk.
Why not just keep it in a box? Or at least a safety curtain.

Gallery 27 instantly clicked for me as a thing that must exist. Art is a common enough skip sub-type; and with an entire GoI out there trying to be cool of course the Foundation has a specialist facility for it. The site director's title is Curator; the staff's greatest collective fantasy is showing everyone who made fun of their art history degree what they actually do for a living. The one building in the world where knowing the difference between Manet and Monet is sometimes the difference between life and death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's in the Museum of Modern Art in New York City. Not on display right now, unfortunately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This painting actually exists, all though the memetic stuff does not. I must find this in some art gallery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually….really want a copy of this painting….I love surreal eyes in places there shouldn't be eyes. Surreal art is some of the best stuff out there. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ohgodgetthateyeoffmyscreen…

*Shudder*

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Our art class just started a surrealism unit. Guess whose work keeps showing up in the examples.
I'm showing this to my teacher when I get the chance. I already showed him 173 (I referenced 173 in a project anyway and it was originally some modern art piece used for the image), so I think he'd like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You just had to make my favorite artist contribute an SCP…take your upvote and darken not my door again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate that picture. Hate it hate it hate it hate it…
I think I got infected by that creepy pic. +1.
Did I mention how much I hate that pic? Because I do. A LOT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read his mention of 'preformed' and instantly panicked, because I only just corrected that and figured I'd mistakenly corrected something that was integral to the working of the article.

Anyway, I'm not sure why this is at 72. Not to impune the writing, that's up the usual Sorts standard. But a painting that makes you paranoid? It's not bad, but I don't really see a reason to upvote unless someone points out something I've missed…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you spot a spelling error (I screw up preform and perform all the time) you can just fix it. Minor grammar edits can be done by anyone, just don't make any substantive edits. The non-existent document was intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly. Sometimes it adds interesting background to an SCP to know that something exists, but the actual details aren't interesting enough to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing all the no about the rule comment. And then adding a good 50 of my own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should be against site rules to cite a supplementary article that doesn't exist.

It would be difficult for me to disagree with this more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know why those are done? Because the information in them is IRRELEVANT! It does NOTHING to know the changes made to that painting. The document would exist for refrences in case of breached containment. Or other possible issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Log in on Page OO-135, or "The Page of Official Complaints Regarding Gullability".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should be against site rules to cite a supplementary article that doesn't exist.

No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
refer to Document 099b

Where? I don't see a…. nein Nein NEEEEIIINNNN! It should be against site rules to cite a supplementary article that doesn't exist.

On a side note,

Recovery was preformed by

I needn't explain that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the Foundation has art galleries? And the painting in MoMA is a forgery? Loving it.

And then:
Weaponized Replication: [DATA EXPUNGED]

Wait, what?
Take your +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, but my favorite part it's "Weaponized replication", and let the imagination do the rest, yeah, I like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite surrealist artist and a wonderfully creepy SCP come together in, what I consider, a perfect blend. I can not upvote this hard enough!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes indeedy, Lumancer would be right that a meme would be necessarily communicable… memetics is all about communication after all, and how information evolves in communication. (edit: that wouldn't mean someone who's been exposed would have to be able to expose others. People can share information, but telling someone about a spooky painting is not the same as seeing it yourself)

The paint and the canvas are not magical, and an exact duplicate would have the same effect. Removing or obscuring certain details dilutes or removes the effect, which is why a small photo of the original does not harm anyone and why a fake, safe replica could be presented to the public without anyone being the wiser.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't? I mean, you can take a picture and show it to someone, and they'll also be infected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well other people can see it can't they? I felt like voicing my thoughts, not looking for an answer to a question I didn't ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you do realize that this post is nearly two years old, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Mona Lisa is the best example I have observed to prove that for most people, art is a sideshow. A few years ago while in Paris with my dad, we went to dozens of art galleries and churches, including the Louvre. Travelling through the museum was relaxing and easy since the building is so massive and there were only two or three other people in each room at most… Then we got to Mona. She was the only painting in the room and it was the size of a lecture hall. PACKED TO THE BRIM WITH PEOPLE. I don't know how they dont consider it a fire hazard. The surreality of walking around a massive nearly empty museum of world renown only to find a single massive room with a single painting housing at least half the full attendance of the entire building deserves it's own bloody SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhere along the line I got the impression (misconception?) that memes were necessarily communicable. Since this isn't, I assumed this was a cognitohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Because it causes psychological changes through visual stimulus that can be reproduced?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh…forgive me if I'm missing something, but how is this memetic?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
yes, cock waffle.

This article is so much better since the edits.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Eye pancake?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better, and not just because it's no longer the Mona Lisa. It's a hell of a lot more manageable length, I really like how it ties into a real artist and his works, and it still keeps the original idea intact. Excellent save, Sorts, +2 swing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorts's changes make this one a lot better. Downvote retracted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Must keep eyes closed, freaky eye will see me….

+1. I see absolutely nothing scary about the Mona Lisa, but this is much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A friend of mine had suggested this painting to get the SCP treatment outside of this article, with the idea that the Foundation should have an entire gallery of forbidden art stashed somewhere… works that either have paranormal properties or extreme memetic effects. So if anyone else wants to add other pieces to Gallery 27 here go nuts.

The original author's idea was solid, IMO, believing that you could see people staring at you through the backs of their heads gave me the heebie jeebies. Now that this isn't MonaLisaLol I hope it recovers from the downvote pit.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Grannted.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I love magritte.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
More like me getting swamped at work and having a social event this evening. Please give me till Saturday to clean up the folderol!

In the meantime here was my thought for a replacement painting:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Portrait_%28painting%29

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Cue exciting music.
Also a montage of Sorts frantically typing, wiping brow, pacing, pumping weights….

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: You have 24 hours.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
This one actually gave me chills the first time around. Maybe give someone a chance to change it and make it not the Mona Lisa?

Hell, I actually have an obscure painting somewhere that could replace it. *dramatic fist shake, tearful eyes* Give me twenty four hours, you monsters!

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: After several months of ehn, it's finally drifted down to -5. Suggesting this be deleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it is generally badly written - and the idea that in 500 years no-ones noticed this obvious effect is ridiculous.
to salvage would need a new portrait - something not famous - and a big rewrite.
finding a decent non-famous picture could be tricky

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What got my downvote? Images of the painting exist before the foundation took it and more than that this is one of the single most famous paintings ever. It is also one of the most analysed and studied. This would not work. A nice idea, and it might have worked with a less famous work, but with the Mona Lisa… No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What Gnostalgia said.

It's just a painting, and not Da Vinci's best by far.

And the power being "oh no the mona lisa is looking me oh nooooes" is really narmy.

Anyway, what actually caused me to downvote this article is that I feel the information is all over the place and not arranged correctly. The Description mostly seems to consist of acquisition, for starters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dislike how everybody and his dog gives the Mona Lisa special powers or a hidden secret or a mysterious origin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea, but the Additional Information section needs to be tightened up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I love this one. Delicious paranoia fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I have a desire to see Leonardo Da Vinci make a guest appearance in Scooby Doo…
